q: What are some of the pain points facing customers in the payments, money transfer, and foreign exchange businesses?

options.A: Payments are fast and transparent but suffer from weak encryption and fraud.
options.B: The main issue is that fintech apps lack access to the payment rails entirely.
options.C: Payments are slow, non-transparent, and costly, especially cross-border, with poor service and hidden fees.
options.D: Customers mostly complain about too many free services eroding bank profits.

q: How important are payments for banks and what are the different sources of revenue?

options.A: Payments are minor, generating under 10% of bank revenues, mostly from reinsurance.
options.B: Very important, generating close to 40% of bank revenues from fees and interest, US$1.9 trillion in 2020.
options.C: Important, but revenues come almost entirely from the bid-ask spread on FX.
options.D: Payments generate 40% of revenues, driven mainly by interchange from cross-border wires.

q: What are the payment rails and what is the difference between the large value transfer system and the system for small payments?

options.A: Rails are the physical network routing payments; LVTS handles large payments fast, small-payment systems handle high volumes slowly.
options.B: Rails are software APIs; LVTS handles consumer payments while ACH handles interbank transfers.
options.C: Rails are the credit card networks; LVTS is slower than the small-payment local roads.
options.D: Rails are blockchain ledgers; LVTS and ACH both settle payments intraday via RTGS.

q: Who are the players in the payments ecosystem and what roles do they play?

options.A: Only the issuing bank and card network are needed; gateways and processors are optional add-ons.
options.B: The merchant bank issues the customer's card while the issuing bank provides the POS machine.
options.C: Reinsurers, underwriters, and brokers route transactions between the customer and merchant.
options.D: Customer, issuing bank, card network, merchant bank/acquirer, payment gateway, and processor, plus banks and MSBs.

q: What are the sources of revenue for payment and money transfer companies? Which revenues are fee-based and which are risk-based?

options.A: All revenue is fee-based; interchange, assessment, and the bid-ask spread are the only sources.
options.B: Fee-based from interchange, assessment, processing, and conversion fees; risk-based from FX, liquidity, and credit risk.
options.C: Risk-based from interchange fees; fee-based only from the bid-ask spread on currencies.
options.D: Fee-based from reinsurance premiums; risk-based from processing and assessment charges.

q: What has been the strategy for new entrants into payments and how did they benefit from technological and regulatory changes?

options.A: Start with a single use-case, build a foothold with core customers, then expand to capture more of the value chain.
options.B: Launch a full-service bank immediately to win mainstream customers before any niche wedge.
options.C: Acquire incumbent banks first, then narrow the product offering to a single use-case.
options.D: Focus on reinsurance and trade finance, benefiting mainly from tighter AML regulation.

q: What are examples of pain points in payments and the fintechs that solved them?

options.A: M-PESA created e-commerce trust via escrow, while Alipay democratized transfers for unbanked Kenyans.
options.B: Wise cut remittances to a $3.99 fee, while WorldRemit offered mid-market FX rates.
options.C: Alipay built e-commerce trust, M-PESA democratized transfers, Wise lowered FX costs, WorldRemit cut remittances, Octopus enabled cashless payment.
options.D: Octopus created e-commerce escrow trust, while Root offered fair currency exchange rates.

q: What are the different segments of the insurance market and how can we assess their relative size?

options.A: A US$5.7 trillion industry measured by claims paid, split into life, auto, and reinsurance segments.
options.B: A US$2 trillion industry measured by premiums, split evenly across life, P&C, and health.
options.C: A US$5.7 trillion industry measured by gross written premiums, led by health (42%) over P&C and life.
options.D: A US$5.7 trillion industry measured by gross written premiums: life (42%), P&C (31%), health (27%).

q: What are insurtechs and what has been their experience disrupting the insurance industry?

options.A: Fintechs using mobile, cloud, sensors, data science, and AI to disrupt insurance; experience is rapid growth mixed with many failures.
options.B: Fintechs using only blockchain to disrupt insurance; experience has been uniformly profitable growth.
options.C: Reinsurers using AI to replace brokers; experience has been steady with almost no failures.
options.D: Fintechs exploiting AI and sensors; nearly all insurtech IPOs, including Root, rose strongly post-IPO.

q: What are some examples of pain points solved by different insurtechs in customer-facing and back-office businesses?

options.A: Snapsheet personalizes auto pricing via sensors, while Root automates claims by photo analysis.
options.B: Customer-facing insurtechs personalize products and pricing; back-office insurtechs automate claims and administration.
options.C: Cyence sells renter policies via bots, while Lemonade models cyber risk for P&C insurers.
options.D: All insurtechs focus on back-office claims; none personalize customer-facing products or pricing.
